coredata.dc:title: The cycle stabilities of the as-cast and quenched La2Mg(Ni0.85Co0.15)9M x (M=B, Cr, Ti; x =0, 0.1) hydrogen storage alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yang-huan
Dong, Xiao-ping
Guo, Shi-hai
Wang, Guo-qing
Ren, Jiang-yuan
Wang, Xin-lin
coredata.dc:description: The PuNi3-type La2Mg (Ni0.85Co0.15)9M x (M=B, Cr, Ti; x =0, 0.1) hydrogen storage electrode alloys were prepared by casting and rapid quenching. The electrochemical cycle stabilities and microstructures of the as-cast and quenched alloys were measured and determined. The effects of B, Cr, Ti and rapid quenching on the microstructures and electrochemical properties of the alloys were investigated in detail. The obtained results show that the as-cast alloys are composed of the (La, Mg)Ni3 phase (PuNi3-type) and the LaNi5 phase as well as a small amount of the LaNi2 phase. A trace of the Ni2B phase exists in the as-cast alloy containing boron, and the Ni2B phase in the alloy nearly disappears after rapid quenching. The relative amount of each phase in the alloys changes with varying quenching rate. The addition of B, Cr enhances the cycle stabilities of the alloys, whereas the addition of Ti reduces the cycle stabilities of the alloys. Rapid quenching increases the cycle stabilities of the alloys, the cycle stabilities of the alloys increase monotonously with the increase of the quenching rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: La-Mg-Ni system electrode alloy
Rapid quenching
Microstructure
Cycle stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relationship between the cycle number and the discharge capacity of the alloys: (a) as-cast alloys; (b) as-quenched alloys (20m/s).
The relationship between the cycle number and the discharge capacity of alloys: (a) M0 alloy; (b) MCr alloy.
Quenching rate dependence of the cycle lives of the alloys.
X-ray diffraction patterns of the as-cast and quenched alloys.
Morphologies of the as-cast alloys taken by SEM: (a) M0; (b) MB; (c) MCr; (d) MTi.
Morphologies and SAD of the as-quenched alloys (20m/s) taken by TEM: (a) M0; (b) MB; (c) MCr; (d) MTi.

The granular morphologies of as-cast alloys before and after electrochemical cycle (SEM) (a), (b), (c), (d) Before cycle of M0, MB, MCr and MTi alloys (e), (f), (g), (h) After cycle of M0, MB, MCr and MTi alloys.

article.ce:title: The cycle stabilities of the as-cast and quenched La2Mg(Ni0.85Co0.15)9M x (M=B, Cr, Ti; x =0, 0.1) hydrogen storage alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Functional Material Research, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute, 76 Xuanyuannan Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The PuNi3-type La2Mg (Ni0.85Co0.15)9M x (M=B, Cr, Ti; x =0, 0.1) hydrogen storage electrode alloys were prepared by casting and rapid quenching. The electrochemical cycle stabilities and microstructures of the as-cast and quenched alloys were measured and determined. The effects of B, Cr, Ti and rapid quenching on the microstructures and electrochemical properties of the alloys were investigated in detail. The obtained results show that the as-cast alloys are composed of the (La, Mg)Ni3 phase (PuNi3-type) and the LaNi5 phase as well as a small amount of the LaNi2 phase. A trace of the Ni2B phase exists in the as-cast alloy containing boron, and the Ni2B phase in the alloy nearly disappears after rapid quenching. The relative amount of each phase in the alloys changes with varying quenching rate. The addition of B, Cr enhances the cycle stabilities of the alloys, whereas the addition of Ti reduces the cycle stabilities of the alloys. Rapid quenching increases the cycle stabilities of the alloys, the cycle stabilities of the alloys increase monotonously with the increase of the quenching rate.

ce:section.ce:para: Ni–MH batteries have been used widely by virtue of several of their advantages, such as high capacity, capable of performing a high rate charge–discharge, high resistance to overcharging and over-discharging, a long cycle life, environmental friendliness, and interchangeability with Ni–Cd batteries. AB5-type hydrogen storage alloys have led to large-scale industrialization in many countries, especially in Japan and China [1,2]. In the very recent years, however, the rechargeable Ni–MH cells are encountering serious competition owing to rapid development of Li-ion cells. The Li-ion cells show higher energy density than the Ni–MH cells per unit weight or volume, and the production cost of Ni–MH batteries based on the current hydride technology also limits their widespread applications. Therefore, investigations of new type electrode alloys with higher capacity and longer cycle life are very important to increase the competition ability of Ni–MH batteries in the rechargeable battery field. Several new and good hydrogen storage alloys were reported recently. One of the most promising candidates is the La-Mg-Ni system as regards the capacity. After investigating the electrochemical performances of the La2MgNi9, La5Mg2Ni23 and La3MgNi14-type electrode alloys, Kohno et al. [3] found that the La5Mg2Ni23-type electrode alloy La0.7Mg0.3Ni2.8Co0.5 has a capacity of 410mAh/g and good cycle stability during 30 charge–discharge cycles. Kadir et al. [4] investigated the structure of the RMg2Ni9 (R=La, Ce, Pr, Nd, Sm and Gd) alloys and the result obtained shows that the alloys have the PuNi3-type structure. Hongge Pan et al. [5] investigated the structures and electrochemical performances of the La0.7Mg0.3(Ni0.85Co0.15) x (x =3.15–3.80) alloy system and obtained a maximum discharge capacity of 398.4mAh/g, but the cycle stability of the alloy needs to be improved further. In order to improve further the cycle stability of the PuNi3-type La2Mg(Ni0.85Co0.15)9 electrode alloy, traces of B, Cr or Ti were added and rapid quenching techniques were employed. The effects of the B, Cr, Ti and rapid quenching techniques on the electrochemical performances and microstructures of the alloys were investigated in detail.

The alloys were melted in a vacuum induction furnace in an argon atmosphere using. In order to prevent the volatilization of Mg during melting, a positive argon pressure of 0.1MPa was applied. After induction melting, the melt was poured into a copper mould cooled by water, and a cast ingot was obtained. Part of the as-cast alloys was re-melted and quenched by melt-spinning with a rotating copper wheel. The quenching rate is expressed by the linear velocity of the copper wheel. Flakes of the as-quenched alloys were obtained with quenching rates of 15, 20, 25 and 30m/s. The chemical compositions of the alloys are La2Mg(Ni0.85Co0.15)9M x (M=B, Cr, Ti; x =0 and 0.1). The alloys are represented by M0 when x is 0, the alloys containing B, Cr and Ti are represented by MB, MCr and MTi, respectively. The purity of all the component metals La, Ni, Co, Mg, B, Cr, Ti is at least 99.7wt.%.

A fraction of the as-cast and quenched alloys, which were ground mechanically into powder below 250mesh, was used for the preparation of the experimental electrode. Electrode pellets with 15mm diameter were prepared by mixing 1g alloy powder and 1g Ni powder as well as a small amount of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as binder, and then compressing under a pressure of 35MPa. After drying for 4h, the electrode pellets were immersed in 6M KOH solution for 24h in order to wet fully the electrodes before the electrochemical measurement.

The electrode pellet was fixed on the outgoing line of a negative electrode of an open tri-electrode cell and the electrochemical characteristics were measured. Ni(OH)2/NiOOH is the positive electrode of the experimental cell, Hg/HgO the reference electrode and 6M KOH solution the electrolyte. The voltage between the negative electrode and the reference electrode is defined as the discharge voltage. In every cycle, the negative electrode was charged with constant current of 100mA/g for 5h, resting for 10min and then discharged at 100mA/g to −0.500V cut-off voltage. The environment temperature of the measurement was kept at 30°C.

The samples of the as-cast alloys were directly polished. The samples thus prepared were etched with a 60% HF solution. The morphologies of the as-cast and quenched alloys were examined by SEM. The samples of the as-cast and quenched alloys were pulverized by mechanical grinding, and the sizes of the powder sample particles were less than 70μm. The phase structures of the alloys were determined by X-ray diffractometer of D/max/2400. The diffraction was performed with Cu Kα1 radiation filtered by graphite. The experimental parameters for determining the phase structure are 160mA, 40kV and 10°/min, respectively. The powder samples were dispersed in anhydrous alcohol for observing the grain morphology with TEM, and for determining the crystalline state of the samples by selected area electron diffraction (SAD). The granular morphologies of the alloy electrode before and after electrochemical cycling were observed by SEM in order to reveal the mechanism of the efficiency loss of the alloy electrode.

The cycle life, indicated by N, is characterized by the cycle number after which the discharge capacity of the alloy obtained with a current density of 100mA/g is reduced to 60% of the maximum capacity. The cycle number dependence of the discharge capacities of the as-cast and quenched alloys is shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen from Fig. 1(a) that the slopes of the curves of the as-cast alloys decrease with the addition of B and Cr, and the slope of the curve of the as-cast alloys increases with the addition of Ti. This indicates that the addition B and Cr is favourable for enhancing the cycle stabilities of the as cast alloys, whereas addition Ti is unfavourable. It can bee seen from Fig. 1(b) that the changes of curve slopes of the as-quenched alloys are similar to those of the as-cast alloys. The cycle number dependence of the discharge capacities of the as-cast and quenched M0 and MCr alloys is shown in Fig. 2 . The results given in Fig. 2 show that the slopes of the curves decrease with the increase of the quenching rate. This indicates that rapid quenching can improve the electrochemical stabilities of the alloys. In order to show the effect of the quenching rate on the cycle life of the alloy, the quenching rate dependence of the cycle lives of the alloys is presented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 shows that the cycle lives of the alloys increase with the increase of the quenching rate. When the quenching rate increases from 0 to 30m/s, the cycle lives of the M0, MB, MCr and MTi alloys are enhanced from 72, 87, 87 and 54 cycles to 100, 106, 119 and 76 cycles, respectively. For the as-quenched alloys, the addition of Cr and B improves the cycle lives of the alloys, whereas the addition of Ti decreases the cycle lives.

The XRD patterns of the as-cast and quenched alloys are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4 that all the alloys are composed of the (La, Mg)Ni3 phase, the LaNi5 phase and the LaNi2 phase, and a trace of Ni2B phase exists in the as-cast MB alloy. It can be concluded by comparing the diffraction peaks of the as-cast alloys that the addition of B, Cr and Ti increases slightly the amount of the LaNi2 phase. The quenching rate has an obvious effect on the relative amount of each phase in the alloys. Worthy of remark is that the Ni2B phase in the MB alloys disappears after rapid quenching. It can be derived by comparing the diffraction peaks of the as-cast and quenched alloys that rapid quenching increases the amount of the LaNi2 phase in the M0 alloy, but decrease the amount of the LaNi2 phase in the MB, MCr and MTi alloys. Therefore, it can be concluded that the existent form and distribution of B, Cr and Ti have an important effect on the formation of the LaNi2 phase in the alloys.

The morphologies of the as-cast and quenched alloys were observed by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the grain sizes of the as-cast alloys are very large and the composition homogeneity of the alloys is poor, and all the experimental alloys are of multiphase structure, containing both the (La, Mg)Ni3 phase (white grey regions) and the LaNi5 phase (black grey regions). Because the amount of the LaNi2 phase is small and it affects the (La, Mg)Ni3 phase in the process of growing, it is difficult to see the morphology of the LaNi2 phase. The amount of the Ni2B in the as-cast MB alloy is too small to be distinguished by SEM. The morphologies and the crystalline state of the as-quenched alloys were examined by TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . The results obtained by SAD show that the as-quenched alloys have a multiphase structure and an obvious tendency to amorphous phase formation in the as-quenched M0, MB, MCr alloys, but the as-quenched MTi alloy still has a typical multiphase structure. It indicates that the addition of Ti is unfavourable for the formation of an amorphous phase.

The battery failure is characterized by a decay of the discharge capacity and a decrease of the discharge voltage. It is confirmed in the literatures [6–9] that the fundamental reasons for the capacity decay of the electrode alloy are the pulverization and oxidation of the alloy during charge–discharge cycle. The lattice internal stress and cell volume expansion, which are inevitable when hydrogen atoms enter into the interstitials of the lattice, are the real driving force that leads to the pulverization of the alloy. The actions of B addition on the cycle life of the as-cast alloy mainly include two aspects. On the one hand, the formation of the Ni2B phase increases the amount of phase boundary, which probably acts as a buffer area for release of the stress formed in the process of hydrogen absorption. Therefore, the anti-pulverization capability of the as-cast alloy is significantly improved. On the other hand, the addition of B can promote the formation of the LaNi2 phase in the as-cast alloy. The lanthanum in the LaNi2 phase is eroded by the alkaline electrolyte during the electrochemical cycle. This leads to an increase of the nickel content in the LaNi2 phase. Thus, the electrocatalytic activation of the LaNi2 phase is significantly improved, and the capabilities of hydrogen absorption and desorption of the LaNi2 phase are enhanced. The capacity decay of the alloy can partly be compensated by the power produced by the LaNi2 phase. Similar to B, the addition of Cr and Ti increases the amount of the LaNi2 phase, which is favourable for the cycle life of the alloys, moreover the addition of Cr can improve the anti-corrosion capability of the alloy [6,10,11]. Therefore, the addition of Cr increases the cycle life of the alloy. The cause that addition of Ti decreases the cycle life of the alloy probably is relevant to the fact that addition of Ti decreases the anti-corrosion and oxidation capabilities of the alloy in alkaline electrolyte.

The cycle lives of the alloys increase with increasing quenching rate (Fig. 3). The main reason for enhancing the cycle life of the alloy is the grain refinement and the formation of an amorphous phase produced by rapid quenching. Rapid quenching increases the amount of the LaNi2 phase in the M0 alloy which is a perceptible factor for rapid quenching enhancing the cycle life of the M0 alloy. The main reason of as-quenched MB and MCr alloys for having longer cycle lives is that rapid quenching leads to the formation of an amorphous phase. The addition of Ti is unfavourable for the formation of an amorphous in the alloys. Therefore, it can be concluded that the main cause that rapid quenching enhances the cycle life of the MTi alloy is the grain refinement resulting from rapid quenching.

In order to understand correctly the mechanism of the efficacy loss of the alloy electrode, the morphologies of the as-cast and quenched alloy particles before and after electrochemical cycle as observed by SEM are shown in Fig. 7 . The result obtained shows that the mechanism of the efficacy loss of the La-Mg-Ni system electrode alloy is different from that of the AB5-type alloy electrode. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that the particle sizes and surface morphologies of the alloys have obviously changed after electrochemical cycling. This indicates that the pulverization of alloy particles takes place during the electrochemical cycle, but the pulverization degree of the alloys is much smaller than that of the AB5-type alloy [12]. Worthy of remark is that an oxidation and corrosion layer, which baffles the diffusion of hydrogen atoms in alloys and decreases the dynamical property of hydrogen absorption and desorption of the alloys, can be seen on the surface of the alloy. It is the oxidation and corrosion layer that leads to the efficacy loss of the alloy. Therefore, it can be concluded that an effective approach of enhancing the cycle life of the PuNi3-type La-Mg-Ni hydrogen storage electrode alloys is to improve the anti-corrosion and oxidation capabilities of the alloys in alkaline electrolyte.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The as-cast and quenched La2Mg(Ni0.85Co0.15)9M x (M=B, Cr, Ti; x =0, 0.1) alloys are composed of the (La, Mg)Ni3 phase (PuNi3-type) and the LaNi5 phase as well as a small amount of the LaNi2 phase. A trace of the Ni2B phase exists in the as-cast MB alloy, and the Ni2B phase in the alloys nearly disappears after rapid quenching. The relative amount of each phase in the alloys depends on the composition of the alloy and the rapid quenching technique. Rapid quenching increases the amount of the LaNi2 phase of the M0 alloy and decreases amount of the LaNi2 phase in the MB, MCr and MTi alloys.

The addition of Cr and B increases the cycle lives of the alloys, but the addition of Ti decreases the cycle lives of the alloys. The cycle life of the alloy increases with the increase of the quenching rate. It is mainly ascribed to the grain refinement and the formation of an amorphous phase produced by rapid quenching.

The tendency to pulverization of the PuNi3-type La-Mg-Ni alloy electrodes is less than that of the AB5-type alloy electrodes. The main reasons of leading to the efficacy loss of the La-Mg-Ni system alloy electrode are the oxidation and corrosion of the alloy electrode surface in alkaline electrolyte in the process of electrochemical cycle. Therefore, an effective approach of enhancing the cycle life of the La-Mg-Ni system alloy is to improve the anti-corrosion and oxidation capability of the alloy in alkaline electrolyte.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundations of China (50131040).
